coredata.dc:description: This study explores a green hydrothermal process for synthesizing carbon quantum dots (CQD) from Bixa orellana seeds, for the dual-mode detection of Cu(II) ions. The facile-prepared CQD was morphologically and optically characterized using various techniques. The CQD exhibited a quasi-spherical shape (3.50 nm) and excitation-dependent photoluminescence with a quantum yield of 10.88 %. The amorphous structure was confirmed via XRD, with Raman spectrum showing weak graphitic features. Cu(II) detection was achieved through fluorescence quenching and electrochemical current enhancement in an extensive linear range, offering low detection limits of 12.82 nM and 6.35 nM, respectively. These findings highlight the potential of Bixa orellana seed derived CQD for sensitive environmental monitoring, while also demonstrating a sustainable approach to repurposing dry plant seeds by eco-friendly nanomaterial development. Additionally, the antioxidant properties of the CQD were also evaluated.

head.ce:title: From seeds to sensors: Bixa orellana derived carbon quantum dots for efficient dual way detection of Cu(II) ion and antioxidant efficacy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study explores a green hydrothermal process for synthesizing carbon quantum dots (CQD) from Bixa orellana seeds, for the dual-mode detection of Cu(II) ions. The facile-prepared CQD was morphologically and optically characterized using various techniques. The CQD exhibited a quasi-spherical shape (3.50 nm) and excitation-dependent photoluminescence with a quantum yield of 10.88 %. The amorphous structure was confirmed via XRD, with Raman spectrum showing weak graphitic features. Cu(II) detection was achieved through fluorescence quenching and electrochemical current enhancement in an extensive linear range, offering low detection limits of 12.82 nM and 6.35 nM, respectively. These findings highlight the potential of Bixa orellana seed derived CQD for sensitive environmental monitoring, while also demonstrating a sustainable approach to repurposing dry plant seeds by eco-friendly nanomaterial development. Additionally, the antioxidant properties of the CQD were also evaluated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, nanomaterials studies have witnessed remarkable advancements driven by the pursuit of sustainable and environmentally friendly synthesis methods. Zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQD), an exciting group of carbon-based nanoparticles with inimitable properties and potential applications have attained substantial attention in various fields [1,2]. In this background, the present study emphases on the greener hydrothermal carbonization process for synthesizing CQD using Bixa orellana (BO) seeds, presenting a facile and sustainable approach to nanoparticle preparation. The enhanced biocompatibility, quantum yield, and photoluminescence properties of fluorescent CQD, along with low toxicity and ease of preparation, make them suitable candidates for a plethora of applications in various fields [2,3]. The sensing applications of CQD arise from their unique optical and electronic characteristics. As CQD are more prominent fluorescent materials than heavy metal quantum dots and organic dyes, their fluorescent nature can be successfully applied to develop fluorescent sensor probes to detect contaminants. The unique optical features and enhanced electrical properties of CQD can be utilized to fabricate efficient electrochemical detection platforms for heavy metal ions.

The precursors for CQD preparation can be switched from toxic and high-cost small organic chemicals to widely available and safe natural precursors. Over the years, silk [4], peanut shells [5], nutmeg seeds [6], milk [7], plant leaves [8–11], etc, were utilized as natural precursors for CQD synthesis. Using plant seeds as precursors for CQD synthesis opens a new way to reuse and recycle leftover biomass. Bixa orellana, commonly known as annatto, is a tropical plant with seeds recognized for diverse applications, including as a natural dye and a traditional medicinal agent [12]. It belongs to the Bixaceae family, and the seeds of BO contain carbon-rich chemical components such as a red color carotenoid called bixin. Approximately two dozen constituents have been isolated from annatto seeds till now. Constituents like beta-carotene, norbixin, isobixin, orellin, bixol, etc, form the carbon source suitable for CQD synthesis [13]. The seeds of BO can act as an exceptional natural green precursor for synthesizing CQD without added passivating agents, as they are inexpensive and readily available. Using plant-based precursors for nanomaterial synthesis aligns with the principles of green chemistry, emphasizing the importance of minimizing environmental impact throughout the entire life cycle of a product. Connecting the inherent properties of these seeds for the synthesis of CQD not only presents an eco-friendly alternative but also explores the potential of agricultural waste for nanomaterial production. The hydrothermal carbonization process employed in this study stands out as an environmentally benign method for converting biomass into carbon-based materials. It involves biomass reaction with water at elevated temperatures and pressures, forming carbonaceous materials. The simplicity and efficiency of this technique make it an attractive choice for the preparation of CQD. Moreover, using Bixa orellana seeds as the precursor adds a layer of sustainability to the process, contributing to the exploration of innovative avenues for the reutilization of agricultural by-products.

Cu(II) ions play a vital role in living organisms as they are third among the most copious heavy metal ion in the living body. The buildup of Cu(II) ions within the ecological environment causes irreparable contamination, which puts the health of living beings in grave danger [14]. Considering the hazardous effects, 20 μM is the least acceptable level of Cu(II) ions in drinking water as determined by the Environmental Protection Agency. Monitoring heavy metal ions using conventional means suffers from tedious experimental procedures and the high cost of instrumentation. Simple, sensitive, and selective detection of metal ions can be achieved by using CQD. Wang et al. fabricated CQD from 1,2,4-triaminobenzene in NaOH for the fluorescence and colorimetric detection of Cu(II) ions [15]. A work by Sonaimuthu et al. reported the fluorescence detection of cupric ions using boron, nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus co-doped CQD [16]. Zhao et al. synthesized CQD from p-phenylenediamine and polyethylene glycol and used them to detect Cu(II) ions using colorimetric and fluorescent methods [17]. But in this work, the innovative application of Bixa orellana seed-derived CQD for detecting Cu(II) ions can be done using fluorescence quenching and electrochemical current enhancement, providing dual detection modes for enhanced reliability. The widespread linear range and low LOD achieved in fluorescence and electrochemical sensing methods underscore the versatility and sensitivity of the Bixa orellana seed-derived CQD in detecting Cu(II) ions. In addition to this, the visible sensing application and the antioxidation activity of CQD are also explored.

In conclusion, this study presents a pioneering approach to synthesizing carbon quantum dots using Bixa orellana seeds through a greener hydrothermal carbonization process for the first time. The comprehensive characterization of the nanoparticles using XRD, XPS, FT-IR, UV–vis., Raman, photoluminescence spectroscopy, and TEM analysis elucidate their structural and optical properties, laying the foundation for understanding their potential applications. The innovative use of these CQD for the targeted and precise detection of Cu(II) ions further underscores their practical utility in environmental monitoring and analytical chemistry. There are no reported works on the fluorescence and electrochemical dual-way detection of Cu(II) ions using green CQD. Overall, this research opens new avenues for the sustainable utilization of plant seeds, contributing to developing eco-friendly nanomaterials with diverse applications.

Seeds for CQD synthesis were collected from Bixa orellana plant in the Mahatma Gandhi University garden (Kottayam, Kerala, India). Analytical grade chemicals used in the study were procured from Merck (India) and were used with no additional purification. Disodium phosphate (Na2HPO4), monosodium phosphate (NaH2PO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were utilized to prepare phosphate pH buffer solutions (PBS). Quinine sulfate was used for quantum yield measurement. For the sensing studies, metal salts such as lead chloride (PbCl2), mercuric chloride (HgCl2), copper chloride (CuCl2), ferrous sulphate (FeSO4), ferric chloride (FeCl3), manganese chloride (MnCl2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), cobalt chloride (CoCl2), nickel chloride (NiCl2), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), and cadmium chloride (CdCl2) were used. 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) was used for the antioxidant activity analyses. Double distilled water was used throughout the procedures, including preparing aqueous solutions.

A wide spectrum of characterization methods was performed to analyze the prepared CQD. For obtaining the FT-IR spectrum, a Perkin Elmer-400 spectrometer having an ATR attachment was used. The UV–vis. spectroscopic study was executed using a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer. All the fluorescence measurements were taken using a Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrofluorometer. For the X-ray diffraction analysis, a PANalytic X’PERT-PRO X-ray spectrometer was used. XPS study was conducted for analyzing the elemental composition and the study was carried out using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB spectrometer. Raman spectra were attained using a confocal Raman microscope with AFM-WITec Alpha 300 RA and the high-resolution TEM images were acquired using a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope. The hydrodynamic diameter and the zeta potential of the CQD were measured on a Horiba SZ-100 scientific nanoparticle analyzer. For the electrochemical sensing experiments, a Biologic SP 200 electrochemical workstation connected to a computer was used. The workstation setup has a standard three-electrode system comprising of a CQD modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE/CQD) working electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode standard electrode, and a platinum wire as the counter electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Facile synthesis of blue emissive CQD
ce:section.ce:para: A representative synthesis process of the CQD is as follows: 3 g of the finely crushed dry BO seeds were transferred into a beaker. After mixing it with 100 mL distilled water, the whole mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined container. The autoclave is kept in a hot-air oven for 7 h at a 200 0C steady temperature. The hydrothermally obtained product was then naturally cooled down, centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min, and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to get rid of larger particles. The product was then purified using dialysis membrane and freeze-dried. It was then re-distributed in double distilled water to obtain the desired concentration of 0.30 mg/mL. For more investigations, the synthesized nano-scale CQD were stored at 4 °C, which can be stable for more than an year. The CQD synthesis from BO seeds is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The quantum yield (QY) of CQD was determined using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference, which has a known quantum yield of 54 %. The QY of CQD was measured by comparing the fluorescence at 340 nm excitation wavelength using a equation (1). The absorbance was set to less than 0.1 during the measurement to avoid re-absorption by quinine sulfate and CQD. A plot of integrated photoluminescence intensity vs. absorbance as obtained.
QYCQD = QYQS × (μCQD/μQS) × (ηCQD/ηQS)2 (1).

QYCQD is the QY of the CQD, QYQS is the QY of the reference, and μCQD and μQS are the slope of the plot for CQD and reference, respectively. ηCQD and ηQS denote the refractive indices of the CQD sample and the reference, respectively.

The fluorescence quenchometric detection of Cu(II) ions in pH 6 phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was monitored by photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. In a representative examination, 100 μL of CQD solution (0.30 mg/mL) was taken and Cu(II) ion solutions of various concentrations (0–180 nM) were added to this solution and the final solution is diluted to 3 mL using PBS. The photoluminescence intensity was monitored at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm after 10 min incubation at room temperature. The intensity of CQD fluorescence with and without Cu(II) ions was represented as F and F0 at 340 nm, and the analytical response was recorded as F/F0. For the interference studies, heavy metal ion solutions were prepared at concentrations that were five times the concentration of the Cu(II) ion solutions used. All the fluorescence measurements were carried out under the same conditions, and aqueous solutions were used for all the measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CQD modified electrode and the electrochemical measurement
ce:section.ce:para: For electrochemical sensing analysis, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was used as the working electrode, an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, and a platinum wire as the counter electrode. First of all, the surface of the bare GCE was made mirror-like by cautious polishing, and then it was washed with ultrapure water. A CQD-containing solution was coated with nafion to the working GCE surface. After drying the electrode at room temperature, Cyclic voltammetric (CV) runs were recorded. Cyclic voltammetric studies of bare GCE and GCE modified with CQD (GCE/CQD) in the presence of various metal ions were done at the potential range of −0.60 to 0.80 V vs Ag/AgCl at 50 mV/s scan rate. Cu(II) ion solutions with concentrations ranging from 10 to 130 nM were prepared to obtain differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) results. In addition to CV, and DPV studies, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra of both the bare GCE and GCE/CQD were recorded.

A real sample study was executed using tap water and river water to examine the practicability of CQD based sensors in practical sensing applications. For this purpose, the two real samples were first filtered using a 0.22 μm filter membrane. After filtration, the actual samples were spiked with varying analyte amounts. The fluorescence spectra of the Cu(II) ion spiked samples were recorded using CQD as sensing probes. In the electrochemical sensing of real samples, the corresponding current value for each spiked sample is evaluated, and, in this way, the practical viability of the CQD-based sensor is checked.

The antioxidant potential of CQD was assessed using a DPPH assay. Various concentrations of CQD were mixed with 0.5 mL ethanolic DPPH solution (1 mM), with the final volume adjusted to 2 mL using ethanol. After 30 min of incubation in the dark at room temperature, discoloration was studied at 517 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The radical scavenging or antioxidant efficacy of the CQD was then calculated.

AC is the absorbance of the ethanolic DPPH solution without CQD, while AS is the absorbance of the solution with the CQD sample.

Studying the effects of temperature, precursor amount, and reaction periods is required to synthesize CQD with optimal performance. It was found that extending the reaction time to 7 h at 200 °C increased the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3 ). Additionally, the fluorescence intensity and quantum yield increased with raising the temperature to 200 °C. Ultimately, it was determined that the ideal conditions for synthesizing Bixa orellana CQD were 200 °C and 7 h. The aqueous solution of CQD showed a stable homogeneous phase and no appreciable precipitation at room temperature.

It is crucial to examine the stability of CQD’s fluorescence properties under various environments to ensure their successful applicability in practical scenarios. The influence of ionic strength on the PL intensity was investigated using NaCl solutions of different concentrations. As seen in Fig. 4 (a), the CQD exhibited good resistance to high salt concentrations, which enables their use in comparable ion-rich biological systems. Following a 1.50 h continuous exposure to 360 nm UV light, studies revealed that the CQD' fluorescence intensity varied negligibly (Fig. 4(b)). In addition, CQD were stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator to ascertain their shelf life. The sample's PL intensity did not change even after one month storage at room temperature (Fig. 4(c)), demonstrating its strong stability and extended shelf life. After a month, the CQD dispersion stability did not change, suggesting that the solution did not precipitate or undergo a notable color shift. There was no evident aggregation and no appreciable drop in PL intensity, according to the PL spectra. Furthermore, the impact of pH on CQD fluorescence property was explored in a range of PBS (pH = 3.–11.0) (Fig. 4(d)). When the medium's pH was altered, a shift in the PL intensity at a constant emission wavelength was seen. Low fluorescence intensity was seen in the highly acidic and basic pH range. In highly acidic environments, protonation of surface groups like carboxyl (–COOH) and hydroxyl (–OH) on CQD leads to increased non-radiative recombination centers, reducing fluorescence intensity. Similarly, in highly alkaline conditions, deprotonation of carboxyl groups forms carboxylate anions (–COO−), which introduce surface defects and quenching sites, lowering PL intensity [18,19]. Both protonation and deprotonation alter the CQD's electronic structure, resulting in reduced PL intensity. At pH 6.0, the highest level of fluorescence intensity was seen. According to these findings, the ideal pH level for Cu(II) ion detection is 6.0. We examined the fluorescence intensity of CQD in the presence of various metal ions in PBS and found that only Cu(II) ions significantly affected the fluorescence intensity of the CQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the morphology and structure of CQD
ce:section.ce:para: The Fig. 5 (a–d) displays the surface morphological features and particle size distribution of the synthesized CQD obtained from the transmission electron microscopy analysis. The TEM images clearly confirm the quasi-spherical shaped morphology and good dispersibility of the dots (Fig. 5(a)). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern confirms the poor crystallinity of the CQD (Fig. 5(b)) [20]. It was discovered that the lattice spacing was 0.22 nm, indicating the existence of a graphitic carbon (100) plane (Fig. 5(c)) [21–23].Image J software was used to obtain the histogram of the particle diameter of the CQD vs. the particle size distribution area (Fig. 5(d)). The particle size distribution of CQD ranges from 1.0 to 6.0 nm, with an average size of 3.5 nm, confirming their quantum dot status [24].

The different functional groups of CQD that underwent hydrothermal treatment are examined using FT-IR spectra (Fig. 5(e)). The peak at 3328 cm−1 was caused by residual hydroxyl functional groups (C-OH), as shown by CQD [25,26]. The formation of a C-H bond was identified as the presence of a small band detected at 2125 cm−1 due to C-H stretching vibration [27]. Furthermore, three bands that matched the stretching vibrations of C = N/C = O/C = C [28–30], C-N [31], and C-O [32,33], respectively, were found at 1633, 1412, and 1073 cm−1. Consequently, the FT-IR findings show that CQD have hydrophilic groups functionalized on their surface, making them very soluble in water. FT-IR analysis confirms that CQD have surface hydroxyl groups and negatively charged functionalities, enabling their dispersion in water. Raman analysis, a non-destructive method, revealed structural flaws in CQD through two peaks at 1344 cm−1 (D band) and 1567 cm−1 (G band), indicating sp3 and sp2 hybridized vibrations, respectively [34–36]. The ID/IG band intensity ratio measured the disorder and sp3/sp2 carbon ratio in CQD. Based on the result of 0.85, it may be concluded that most of the carbons in CQD are sp2 graphitic carbons with structural flaws. The Raman spectra exhibit a notable tiny peak at around 1430 cm−1, which indicates the presence of C-H vibration [37]. The XRD spectra of CQD showed a broad peak at 23.9° (Fig. 5(g)), similar to the graphite (002) plane, indicating disorganized carbon atoms and an amorphous structure [38].

A clear idea about the chemical bonds present in the CQD and the overall elemental composition of the CQD can be investigated using the XPS analysis. The composition of the CQD consists of atoms of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen as depicted by the discrete peaks at 284.80 [39–41], 531.50 [42,43], and 398.30 eV [44,45](Fig. 6 (a)). A C1s peak was detected, and it was deconvoluted into five peaks with respective energies of 284.50, 284.70, 285.50, 286.30, and 288.10 eV using Gaussian functions (Fig. 6(b)). It is possible to identify C-C [46], C = C [47], C-N [47], C-O-C [48], and C = O [47]as these peaks, respectively. The analysis of four peaks in the N1s spectra at 396.80, 397.40, 398.00, and 398.30 eV suggests the presence of O = C-N [49], C-N [50], C = N-C [49], and C-N-C [51], respectively (Fig. 6 (c)). Gaussian functions were used for the deconvolution of O1s peak to two peaks with binding energy values of 531.00 and 532.50 eV, respectively credited to C-O-C/C-OH [52]and O-H [16] bonds as shown in (Fig. 6(d)). XPS and FT-IR analysis results were consistent.

The dynamic light scattering (DLS) study provides a hydrodynamic diameter of 4.60 nm for the CQD (Fig. 7 (a)), slightly greater than the TEM observation.This difference is typical, as particle size in a vacuum is generally smaller than in a solvent. The enduring colloidal stability and good water dispersibility of the CQD is a result of the vast number of surface functionalities. The zeta potential of –32.80 mV indicates a highly negative surface charge, which contributes to their water solubility (Fig. 7(b)). CQD must have hydrophilic negative charge moieties on their surface in order for them to be highly soluble in water. However, adding Cu(II) reduces the zeta potential, due to interactions between Cu(II) ions and the CQD surface groups.

Fig. 8 (a) and (b), respectively show the absorption and photoluminescence spectra of CQD. The CQD emitted bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, with a strong absorption peak at 276 nm and a visible range tail, indicating n-π* transition of C = O [53,54]. The photoluminescence emission spectra exhibited a broad range of excitation wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm, peaking at 340 nm excitation and 437 nm emission. As the excitation wavelength increased, the fluorescence intensity decreased and redshifted (Fig. 8(c)). This excitation-dependent emission intensity, due to varying surface states and sizes, allows CQD to be used as fluorescence sensors for various applications [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance for Cu(II) ions sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of CQD was tested with various metal ions (Pb(II), Ni(II), Cu(II), Cd(II), Fe(III), Co(II), Fe(II), Hg(II), Mn(II), Cr(VI), and Zn(II)) using PL experiments. The quenching ratio (F/F0) was used to create a histogram (Fig. 9 (a)), where F is the PL intensity with metal ions and F0 without. CQD concentration was kept constant, with a blank control (metal ions absent) for comparison. Only Cu(II) ions significantly quenched the CQD fluorescence, making the sensor specific to Cu(II). In the interference study, tests with various metal ions demonstrated that only Cu(II) notably reduced the fluorescence of CQDs, suggesting the sensor's specificity (Fig. 9(b)). Sensitivity analysis showed that increasing Cu(II) concentrations (0–180 nM) decreased CQD fluorescence (Fig. 9(c)), indicating a “turn-off fluorescence” effect. Our proposed method allows Cu(II) ions to be easily, quickly, sensitively, and selectively detected. This study shows that the PL fluorescence approach may be applied to fluorometric detection off Cu(II) ions. In ideal circumstances, there would be a linear correlation between Cu(II) concentrations and quenching efficiency (F/F0) (Fig. 9(d)) and this can be represented as the equation, F/F0 = 0.0047[Cu(II)](nM) + 1.0257 (R2 = 0.9947). The equations 3sd/m and 10sd/m (where “sd” is the standard deviation of the samples and “m” denotes the slope of a straight line) were used to obtain a detection limit of 12.82 nM and a limit of quantification of 42.74 nM. This experiment showed a low detection limit and a good linear range. It should be noted that this sensor is an affordable and environment friendly solution because it is based on green synthesis of CQD.

The mechanism of CQD fluorescence quenching, classified into static and dynamic quenching, is explained by various methods. Static quenching occurs when a connection is formed between the fluorophore and quencher, whereas collisions cause dynamic quenching [56]. In this investigation, adding Cu(II) ions muted the fluorescence of CQD. The CQD fluorescence quenching is influenced by the interaction between the particles and Cu(II) ions. Static quenching and dynamic quenching are two different categories of quenching mechanisms. Three main processes might be implicated in dynamic quenching: fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), internal filtering effect (IFE), and photo-induced electron transfer (PET). Cu(II) ions' UV–vis. spectra show the existence of a significant absorption band at about 245 nm, which does not overlap with the emission wavelength of CQD at 437 nm (Fig. 10 (a)). This indicates that there is no evidence of Förster resonance energy transfer occurring during the quenching process. When CQD were analyzed using IFE, there was no overlap between the absorption spectrum and emission spectrum of CQD (Fig. 10(b)) [57]. Due to the lack of overlap between these two spectra, the IFE has yet to be considered a plausible explanation. The analysis of the UV–vis. absorbance spectra examined potential interactions between CQD and Cu(II) ions. The UV spectra of CQD + Cu(II) showed a red shift and expanded band at 276 nm, whereas Cu(II) ions showed a d-d transition peak at 245 nm (Fig. 10(c)). This red shift in absorbance spectrum of CQD + Cu(II) indicated interaction between CQD and Cu(II) ions. The fluorescence quenching mechanism involves the movement of electrons from CQD excited states to Cu(II) ion unoccupied orbits. The UV–vis. spectra of CQD and CQD + Cu(II) ions show no significant variation in the 437 nm wavelength (Fig. 10(d)). It validates the static quenching and rules out the possibility of PET quenching. To enhance comprehension of the mechanism responsible for fluorescence quenching, the PL lifetime measurement of CQD was investigated. The linear Stern-Volmer correlations are often linked to the coexistence of static and dynamic quenching. When quenching occurs dynamically, the CQD' photoluminescence lifetimes vary in response to Cu(II) ion concentration. Still, they stay constant when quenching occurs statically (Fig. 10(e)). The time-resolved PL spectra of CQD + Cu(II) ions were 4.98 ns, similar to the 5.00 ns spectra of pure CQD. This implies that static quenching can be a part of the quenching mechanism. The World Health Organization (WHO) states that drinking water should have fewer than 30 nM of Cu(II) ions [58]. The current methodology is a more appropriate analytical approach for Cu(II) detection at shallow concentration levels since the obtained LOD value of the current study in both fluorescence (LOD = 12.82 nM) and electrochemical (LOD = 6.35 nM) measurements falls below this threshold.

The size distribution of particles dispersed in a liquid medium is commonly investigated using DLS measurement. After the introduction of cupric ions, the particle size of CQD increased from 4.60 nm to 34.90 nm (Fig. 11 (a)). In the sensing application, the particle size enhancement of CQD in the DLS measurement after the addition of Cu(II) ions can be attributed either to the aggregation of CQD induced by the interaction of CQD with the analyte ions or to the adsorption/encapsulation of Cu(II) ions onto the CQD surface [59]. Zeta potential analysis results provide more evidence for the connection between Cu(II) and CQD. As was previously mentioned, CQD were found to have a negative charge when their zeta potential was measured at –32.8 V. Nevertheless, the zeta potential value dropped to −10.61 V when Cu(II) ions were added (Fig. 11(b)). This suggests an electrostatic contact between the oppositely charged Cu(II) ions and CQD in the slightly acidic PBS medium. This was attributed to the ground-state complex formation of functional groups on the CQD surface with Cu(II), which protects the CQD' charge [60,61]. These findings validate the specificity and sensitivity of the CQD toward Cu(II) and are in good accord with the quenching efficiencies found in the Stern-Volmer equation.

The glassy carbon electrode modified with CQD in its as-prepared state has been employed as a sensor for Cu(II) ions’ electrochemical sensing. Using a straightforward drop casting technique, the GCE/CQD electrode was created by applying a CQD-Nafion solution to the surface of a glassy carbon electrode and allowing it to dry in the air at ambient temperature. In the electrochemical experiments using metal salt solutions, the CQD-modified GCE electrode was employed as the working electrode in the detection tests. In cyclic voltammetry experiments, a CQD-modified GCE exhibited significant sensitivity to Cu(II) ions compared to other metals, with responses observed in the potential range of −0.60 to 0.80 V vs Ag/AgCl at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. Conversely, the bare GCE did not show any redox peaks with the metal ions (Fig. 12 (a)). The CQD-modified electrode's surface, enriched with imine (C = N-C) and hydroxyl groups, provides binding sites for Cu(II) ions. This interaction enhances both anodic and cathodic currents on the electrode. The detection of Cu(II) is facilitated by the electrode's increased surface area, high electrical conductivity, and electronic properties conferred by the CQDs [62]. The modified electrode exhibited its highest current response at pH 4 when evaluated across different pH levels (Fig. 12(b)). As a result, additional studies were conducted at this optimal pH. The CV curves display well-defined redox peaks across a scan rate range from 10 to 100 mV/s (Fig. 12(c)). Increasing the scan rate results in higher peak currents, suggesting the reaction's reversibility. Impedance spectroscopy is effective for assessing the interfacial electrical properties of a modified electrode. The electron transfer resistance in EIS determines the semicircle diameter of the impedance, which in turn regulates the redox probe's electron transfer kinetics at the electrode surface. The results of impedance spectroscopy for both CQD/GCE and bare glassy carbon electrode are displayed in Fig. 12(d). The bare GCE displays a significant semicircle, whereas, after the casting of CQD into the GCE surface, the plot displays a small resistance value. It is explained by the layer of CQD forming and around the electron transfer. The lower semicircle radius of GCE/CQD displays its small resistance and enhanced electron transfer capacity. It suggests that the GCE/CQD is a superior material for electrical conductivity.

The CV runs of GCE/CQD in the presence of various metal ions (Co(II), Cr(VI), Cu(II), Pb(II), Fe(II), Ni(II), Fe(III), Hg(II), Mn(II), Cd(II), and Zn(II)) is given in Fig. 13 (a). The preferential cupric ion detection was found in cyclic voltammetry analysis because of the particular electron transference interaction between the GCE/CQD surface and analyte ions. The fabricated sensor was utilized to determine the Cu(II) detection limit using DPV. As the concentration of Cu(II) in DPV study increased from 10 nM to 130 nM, there was a corresponding rise in feedback current (Fig. 13(b)). The LOD of Cu(II) was determined to be 6.35 nM based on the linear plot of the Cu(II) ion concentration against peak current (Fig. 13(c)). The quantification limit for electrochemical sensing of Cu(II) ion was found to be 21.17 nM. Selective Cu(II) detection investigation was carried out using diverse metal ions (X) as interferents (Fig. 14 (a)). The sensor stability was investigated by associating CV runs of fresh GCE/CQD sensor and one month old sensor towards Cu(II) ions. In both runs similar peak current was obtained, suggesting the promising stability of the sensor (Fig. 14(b)). The redox process is identified by dual peak currents in the electrochemical sensing: the positive and negative. Cyclic voltammetry shows that Cu(II) reduction on the electrode surface creates a negative peak current, while Cu(0) oxidation creates a positive peak current, due to electron transfer between GCE/CQD and Cu(II) ions (Fig. 15 ). This process enables the electrochemical detection of Cu(II) using the CQD-modified electrode.

The electrochemical sensing mechanism involving cyclic voltammetric current enhancement of GCE/CQD in Cu(II) solution likely involves several steps. The adsorption of Cu(II) on the imine and hydroxyl functionalised CQD surface followed by their coordination interactions can form complexes. The adsorption of ions may facilitate electron transfer resulting in changes in the electrochemical behaviour of the system and thereby enhanced redox reactions. The GCE/CQD based sensor is highly selective towards Cu(II) ions only. Other metal ions may exhibit different electrochemical behaviors or may not form stable complexes with the CQD surface, resulting in lower current enhancement. The suggested sensor's dynamic range and detection limit were contrasted with the other documented techniques for Cu(II) measurement in the literature (Table 2 ).

The Fig. 16 displays findings for the real samples spiked with specific amounts of Cu(II) under ideal circumstances. The results show that this analytical method may be used for the detection of the amount of Cu(II) in actual samples with an acceptable level of accuracy and reliability. The capacity of CQD sensor to perform in an actual environment was assessed by looking for Cu(II) ions in tap water and river water. The tap water from the municipality and water sample from river underwent the standard recovery processes. As demonstrated in Fig. 16, the fluorescence and electrochemical analysis of the genuine samples were assessed using the standard addition approach at four distinct concentrations: 10 μM, 20 μM, 30 μM, and 40 μM at pH 6 and pH 4, respectively. Three times (n = 3) in total, each sample was tested under the same set of conditions. The suggested CQD may also be used for the practical sensing of cupric ions in real-world specimens, as evidenced by the recovery rates in the range of 90–100 % and the relative standard deviation of less than 3 %. According to these results, CQD may be suitable real-world probes for dual-mode detection of Cu(II) ions.

To conclude, we have developed a straightforward, sustainable, low-cost, and single-step hydrothermal preparation of CQD using a seed now used as the novel green feedstock. The use of widely accessible left-over biomass as the carbon precursor leads to an environment friendly method lacking any negative influences. These CQD showed excellent stability and bright blue fluorescence without needing an additional surface passivation agent. The resulting CQD were spherical, with a fine size distribution of about 3.50 nm. Under optimal settings, the interaction between CQD and Cu(II) ions results in an electron transfer that quenches fluorescence. A linear connection was observed between Cu(II) ions concentration (0–180 nM) and the fluorescence quenching efficiency of CQD, F/F0 and detection limit of 12.82 nM was attained. In addition, CQD were also investigated for the electrochemical sensing of Cu(II) ions and obtained a limit of detection 6.35 nM. The practical application analysis of the CQD as electrochemical and fluorescent sensor for Cu(II) ions displayed admirable detection rates. As a result, our research shows that using CQD generated from Bixa orellana seeds as an efficient Cu(II) sensor is a feasible approach. The CQD also displayed a 93 % antioxidantion efficacy in the DPPH assay. These findings not only contribute to the growing domain of knowledge on green synthesis methods for nanomaterials but also highlight the potential of Bixa orellana seed-derived CQD as a selective and sensitive sensing platform for environmental monitoring, particularly in the detection of cupric ions. The study opens new avenues for the sustainable reuse of plant seeds, offering insights into the development of eco-friendly nanomaterials with practical applications in analytical chemistry and environmental science. Overall, this research serves as a stepping stone towards a more sustainable and innovative approach to nanomaterial synthesis and application.

Jincy Mathew: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Bony K John: Visualization, Validation, Data curation. Sneha Mathew: Validation, Data curation. Binila K Korah: Validation, Data curation. Beena Mathew: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization.
